The point is to let other developers know which env vars the project needs without leaking the actual values. Each key usually gets a brief comment explaining it. Example: my open-source
Then load it with the dotenv package. Usage is one line:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Above is Node.js. In PHP it's

Most hosting platforms also have an Environment section in their dashboard where you can set env vars directly.
That's the quick tour of usage.

This is the bit people stumble on. The most common case is sensitive info — keys, passwords, tokens — but you can also stash other commonly-used config in env vars too. Site name, site URL, logo URL — any of those work. Depends on your needs and code structure.
project, I put the OpenAI model name in
. That way switching models is a one-line config change, not a code change.
Env vars cover so many use cases, you can basically think of
Now for the finer details — differences between frontend and backend usage.

Backend first — when your service is deployed, you set env vars in the platform's Environment section. The values get injected into your code at runtime. It's the same shape as your local development setup, just running on the server. And because backend code runs server-side, your env vars are
. I'm emphasizing "very safe" here — I'll come back to why.
Render.com's Environment section as an example:
Frontend devs use env vars too. With Vite, you create a
file and access values like this:
Easy enough. But heads up — frontend env vars are
a place for sensitive info, because frontend code runs in the browser, meaning everything in your bundle is readable.
What does that look like in practice? Say I create a project with
language-vue shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
— and there it is, inlined right into the bundle.

That's why frontend env vars shouldn't hold secrets. Use them for non-secret config — API URLs, site titles, feature flags — stuff that's repeated across the app and might change later. That's also why I said "backend env vars are very safe" earlier — backend runs on the server, frontend runs in the browser, so anyone who downloads your bundle can see your frontend env values.
Quick recap of what to remember:
doesn't go in version control. Commit only
(with empty values) and document the setup in your README.
Don't commit secrets — database passwords, API keys, etc. — make sure
Frontend env vars shouldn't hold secrets, because the browser sees everything.
That's about it for env vars.

A quick rundown of what .env is and when it makes sense to use one. Covers what env vars are for, the common file conventions (.env.development, .env.production, .env.example), how to load them with dotenv, when to use env vars beyond secrets, and the critical caveat that frontend env vars are NOT a place to store sensitive info — they end up in your built bundle.
